notes.anchor: I bore in mind my son-in-law
notes.note: Cicero's son-in-law was P. Cornelius Dolabella, Tullia's husband, who had thrown in his lot with Caesar; the kinship is Cicero's standing excuse to Caesar's men for not joining Pompey outright.

notes.anchor: run about with lictors against my will
notes.note: Lictors were the attendants who carried the fasces before a magistrate or promagistrate; Cicero, still holding imperium from his Cilician command and awaiting a triumph, was obliged to keep them, which made discreet movement impossible.

notes.anchor: There you have a Spartan scytale
notes.note: The scytale was a Spartan device for sending secret dispatches: a strip wound on a rod of matching diameter so the message read only when rewound. Cicero applies the term ironically to Antony's terse, coded refusal.
Roman dates were counted backward and inclusively from three fixed points each month; the fifth day before the Nones of May is 3 May. Cicero glosses it himself as "today."

notes.anchor: carrying Cytheris around with him in an open litter, like a second wife
notes.note: Volumnia Cytheris was a celebrated freedwoman mime-actress and Antony's mistress; parading her openly in a litter as though a married matron was a notorious scandal that Cicero recurs to with disgust. The litter was a curtained conveyance carried by bearers, normally reserved for respectable women.
